sources.llm_title: Master of Information Management and Systems
sources.llm_summary: Program page for UC Berkeley’s MIMS, describing it as a two-year, STEM-designated, full-time professional master’s program and identifying the awarded credential.
Degree-requirements page stating that the MIMS program is a two-year full-time degree program requiring at least 48 semester units of study.

Paths page describing the degree as flexible and individualized, with common focus areas such as human-computer interaction and user experience design, applied data science and machine learning, information policy and ethical technology, and social and technical innovation in entrepreneurship.

llm_rationales.note: Selected interdisciplinary, professional, digital, and UI/UX because the page says the program is a 'two-year STEM-designated, full-time professional master’s program' for 'aspiring technology leaders, AI engineers, UX designers, and data-driven decision-makers,' and that it 'bridges technology, people, and policy.'

UI/UX is directly supported by the paths page, which lists 'human-computer interaction and user experience design' as common focus areas and describes students conducting user research and designing user interfaces.

Did not add stem_adjacent or social_impact because the evidence suggests both, but the strongest directly stated themes were professional, interdisciplinary, digital, and UI/UX; kept to the four most salient tags.
The page metadata and description state that MIMS is a 'two-year' full-time program and the schema.org JSON-LD lists timeToComplete as 'P2Y'.
Two years at Berkeley's standard two-semester academic year corresponds to four semesters, so the appropriate category is four_semesters.
Used the program-stated estimated total tuition and fees for the degree for a new student starting Fall 2025: $82,096. This is tuition and fees only, suitable for cross-program comparison.
Used the same program-stated estimated total tuition and fees figure of $82,096 in USD as the total degree cost in base currency.

The MIMS funding page snippet shows bundled cost-of-attendance figures including personal expenses and total expected graduate costs, but no isolated tuition-and-fees subtotal was available in the provided evidence, so it was not used for comparison_cost_usd.
The fetched materials do not name any specific required courses or course codes, only high-level program structure and duration.
The fetched materials describe possible focus areas but do not provide a formal elective count, unit rule, or bucket structure.
The page is branded UC Berkeley School of Information and the structured data provider is UC Berkeley School of Information.
The page title and structured data name the program as Master of Information Management and Systems.
The structured data explicitly lists the educationalCredentialAwarded as Master of Information Management and Systems.
This is a master's-level professional degree program, so degree_type is master's.
The program is housed in UC Berkeley’s School of Information.
The School of Information is best mapped to the computing_hosted model because it is an information-technology-oriented academic unit rather than a design, business, or arts school.
UC Berkeley is located in Berkeley, California, United States.

identity.program_name: Master of Information Management and Systems
identity.credential_name: Master of Information Management and Systems

positioning.positioning_summary: MIMS is positioned as a two-year, full-time professional master’s program for aspiring technology leaders and information professionals, with an explicitly interdisciplinary curriculum bridging technology, people, and policy. The program also emphasizes user experience, human-computer interaction, data science, information policy, and innovation, with clear social-impact and digital-product orientation.

curriculum.curriculum_summary: UC Berkeley’s MIMS is a two-year, full-time, onsite, STEM-designated professional master’s program requiring 48 credits. The supplied materials do not show a full requirement table or named required courses, but they do indicate that students follow individualized paths and may focus on areas such as human-computer interaction and user experience design, applied data science and machine learning, information policy and ethical technology, and social and technical innovation in entrepreneurship. The excerpt also indicates the program includes final projects.
curriculum.elective_requirements: The supplied materials do not state a formal elective count or unit rule. They describe the degree as flexible and individualized, with common focus areas including human-computer interaction and user experience design, applied data science and machine learning, information policy and ethical technology, and social and technical innovation in entrepreneurship, and more.

curriculum.evidence_curriculum_summary:
### Source: https://www.ischool.berkeley.edu/programs/mims/degreerequirements

The page identifies MIMS as the Master of Information Management and Systems and says it is a two-year full-time degree program. The page title is MIMS Degree Requirements, but the excerpt provided does not include a full requirement table or a complete list of courses; it does, however, contain one concrete curriculum fact and a few structural signals.

The only explicit program-length and pace statement on the page is that the MIMS program is “a two-year full-time degree program.” No part-time option, modality detail, or credit total appears in the excerpt.

The page text shown does not include named required courses, elective lists, unit counts, sequencing by term, thesis/capstone/internship language, concentration or track options, or policies about taking courses outside the department. It also does not show advising, milestones, or learning outcomes in the excerpt provided.

Because the page is a degree-requirements page, the strongest curriculum-related fact available here is simply the program’s overall structure as a two-year full-time master’s degree. No additional degree-completion rules are visible in the supplied text.

---

### Source: https://www.ischool.berkeley.edu/programs/mims/paths

This page is a “Paths Through the MIMS Degree” page for UC Berkeley’s School of Information, and it gives only high-level curriculum-path information rather than a formal requirement list. The central curriculum fact stated on the page is that every MIMS student’s degree is unique. It frames the degree as flexible and individualized, with common focus areas including human-computer interaction and user experience design, applied data science and machine learning, information policy and ethical technology, and social and technical innovation in entrepreneurship, “and more.”

The page does not list specific required courses, course codes, unit totals, sequencing, or term-by-term milestones. It also does not name any capstone, thesis, internship, studio, or fieldwork requirement on the excerpt provided. What it does provide is a description of possible study directions within the degree, implying that students can shape their coursework around a chosen focus area.

The focus areas explicitly named on the page are:
human-computer interaction and user experience design;
applied data science and machine learning;
information policy and ethical technology;
social and technical innovation in entrepreneurship.

Because the page says “Every MIMS student’s degree is unique,” the strongest curriculum signal here is that the program supports individualized paths rather than a single fixed sequence. The page also suggests breadth beyond the listed examples by ending with “and more,” but it does not enumerate additional tracks or concentrations.

---

### Source: https://www.ischool.berkeley.edu/programs/mims

The page identifies the program as the Master of Information Management and Systems, or MIMS, at UC Berkeley’s School of Information. It describes MIMS as a two-year, STEM-designated, full-time professional master’s program. The structured data on the page also specifies that the program is onsite, takes two years to complete, has two terms per year, and requires 48 credits. The credential awarded is the Master of Information Management and Systems.

Beyond those high-level facts, the excerpt provided does not include a detailed curriculum map, course sequence, concentration list, or explicit graduation requirements. No named courses, course codes, electives, capstone, thesis, internship, studio, field experience, or advising milestones appear in the visible text. The page does, however, frame the program as intended for aspiring technology leaders, AI engineers, UX designers, and data-driven decision-makers, and says graduates “drive innovation, influence policy, and design products that improve lives,” which gives some sense of the program’s applied, professional orientation.

The only concrete curriculum-structure details visible are:
- Degree: Master of Information Management and Systems
- Program length: two years
- Pace/mode: full-time, onsite
- Designation: STEM-designated
- Credit total: 48 credits
- Academic calendar detail: two terms per year

No other curriculum requirements are stated in the excerpted page text.

---

### Source: https://www.ischool.berkeley.edu/programs/mims/projects/2026

This page is a “MIMS Final Projects” page for UC Berkeley School of Information, and the curriculum-related information it contains is limited to the existence of a final-project component for the MIMS program. The page title and metadata identify the page as “MIMS Final Projects,” which strongly indicates that MIMS students complete final projects as part of the degree experience. However, in the excerpt provided, there are no visible details about the project format, credit value, timing, sequencing, supervision, evaluation, or whether the final project is required for all students.

The page text shown does not include degree length, total units or credits, required courses, electives, tracks, concentrations, prerequisites, co-requisites, term-by-term sequencing, thesis language, internship or fieldwork requirements, or any named course codes and titles. It also does not provide learning outcomes, advising milestones, or policies about outside-department electives.

The only curriculum-relevant fact that can be stated from the page excerpt is that the MIMS program has final projects, and that this page is specifically devoted to them.
